error.signature: SSL: CERTIFICATE_VERIFY_FAILED - unable to get local issuer certificate, even though cert is valid

verdict.summary: TLS requires the full certificate chain: leaf cert -> intermediate(s) -> root CA. Browsers often cache intermediates and work anyway, but programmatic clients (curl, Python requests, Node.js) fail. The server must send intermediates; the client only trusts root CAs.

dead_ends.action: Install leaf certificate only, assuming clients have intermediate certs
dead_ends.why_fails: Browsers cache intermediate certs from previous visits. curl, Python, and Node.js don't cache intermediates. They fail on every connection.

dead_ends.action: Add root CA cert to the server's chain file
dead_ends.why_fails: Root CA should NOT be in the chain. Clients already have root CAs in their trust store. Including it wastes bandwidth and can confuse some TLS implementations.

dead_ends.action: Test TLS with a browser and assume all clients will work
dead_ends.why_fails: Browsers have intermediate certificate caching (AIA fetching). Programmatic clients don't. Browser testing misses missing intermediate cert issues.

workarounds.action: Include full chain in order: leaf, intermediate(s), but NOT root
workarounds.how: cat server.crt intermediate.crt > fullchain.pem  # leaf first, then intermediate(s)

workarounds.action: Test with openssl s_client or SSL Labs, not just a browser
workarounds.how: openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -showcerts  # verify full chain is sent

workarounds.action: Use ACME/certbot which automatically includes the correct chain
workarounds.how: certbot uses fullchain.pem by default which includes leaf + intermediates in correct order
